rationales.E: Simultaneous administration has lower efficacy than the 36-48 hour interval; the gap is required for mifepristone-induced cervical priming and progesterone receptor antagonism. Same-visit administration is correct only in selected emergency contexts where 36-48 hours wait is impossible (MS-2 Step PI; RANZCOG TOP).

rationales.A: Haemodynamic compromise plus sepsis signs plus active bleeding makes outpatient management unsafe with inadequate source control. The anti-pattern is treating a septic patient as outpatient. Outpatient miso + doxy is correct only in stable patients with incomplete TOP and no sepsis (RANZCOG TOP; eTG Antibiotic).
rationales.C: Oxytocin has limited effect on a non-puerperal uterus; antibiotics should not wait for cultures in sepsis (ANZICS 1-hour rule); source control must not be delayed. Oxytocin-only is correct in atonic PPH at term, not in septic abortion (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).
rationales.D: TXA is adjunctive but does not address source; delaying antibiotics breaches the 1-hour sepsis standard. TXA is correct as adjunct to source control plus antibiotics, not as sole therapy (eTG Sepsis; ANZICS Sepsis).
rationales.E: Methotrexate addresses neither retained products nor sepsis, and is contraindicated in active infection. The anti-pattern is using MTX for any pregnancy-related bleeding. MTX is correct only for unruptured ectopic without infection (AMH; eTG).

rationales.A: Frequency and verbal abuse are markers of abuse but are not the specific extreme-risk discriminators; the cluster in option C predicts lethality more strongly. Verbal abuse alone is correct as a marker of IPV but not as an extreme-risk indicator (1800RESPECT; ANROWS).
rationales.B: Historical low-severity violence is concerning but not in the same risk class as recent strangulation + threats + escalation in pregnancy + firearm access. Historical low-severity is correct as background context, not as extreme-risk indicator (ANROWS).
rationales.D: The act of disclosure is positive engagement, not a risk marker; risk is determined by the content. Disclosure itself signals trust, not danger (ANROWS).
rationales.E: Lack of AVO is common and does not by itself stratify extreme risk. Absence of legal protection raises practical concerns but is not predictive of femicide independently of the danger features (1800RESPECT).

rationales.A: Mandated reporters cannot suspend the legal duty at the patient's request; the threshold ('risk of significant harm') has been reached. The anti-pattern is conflating therapeutic alliance with legal obligation. Therapeutic-trust framing is correct in informing patients about the report, not in declining to make it (NSW MRG; NSW Children and Young Persons Act 1998).
rationales.B: Behavioural change in the context of witnessed IPV meets the NSW Risk of Significant Harm threshold and triggers mandated reporting. Reporting-only-to-GP is correct only for concerns below RoSH threshold (NSW MRG).
rationales.D: ADVO applications are typically made by police or the protected person; clinicians can encourage and support but the immediate mandated step is child protection reporting because children are involved. Police-direct contact is correct in immediate physical-threat situations (NSW MRG; NSW Police ADVO guidance).
rationales.E: Delaying a RoSH report once threshold is met breaches the mandated reporter duty. Delay is essentially never correct once RoSH threshold is crossed (NSW MRG).

rationales.B: The honeymoon phase predictably gives way to renewed tension and incident phases; reassurance and abandoning the plan is unsafe. Reassurance is correct only when the abuser has engaged with intervention and tracked behavioural change is sustained (RACGP; WHO).
rationales.C: Leaving is the highest-risk time for homicide; her autonomy and timing must be respected with safety planning. Insistence-on-leaving is correct only with consent-based safety planning toward that goal (ANROWS).
rationales.D: Joint counselling is contraindicated in active IPV; it provides the perpetrator a platform and worsens risk. Joint counselling is correct only in non-violent relationship conflict, never in active IPV (WHO; RACGP).
rationales.E: Ongoing follow-up is recommended through cycles; discharging removes support at the highest-risk time. Discharge is correct only when patient is consistently safe with established external support (RACGP).

rationales.A: Levonorgestrel EC efficacy is limited to within 96 hours of unprotected intercourse; not first-line beyond that window. LNG-EC is correct within 72-96 hours of exposure, not beyond (Family Planning Australia; eTG).
rationales.B: Ulipristal extends the window to 120 hours but evidence supports efficacy up to 120 hours, and at exactly 120 hours efficacy is borderline; copper IUD is more reliable. UPA is correct up to 120 hours post-coital when IUD is not desired (Family Planning Australia).
rationales.D: HIV PEP must be commenced within 72 hours of exposure; at 120 hours the window has closed. PEP is correct only within 72 hours of exposure (ASHM PEP Guidelines).
rationales.E: She has documented completed primary hepatitis B course; check anti-HBs titre rather than re-vaccinate. Re-vaccination is correct only if titre is sub-protective (<10 mIU/mL) and immune memory cannot be relied upon (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

rationales.A: Many DFSA agents (notably GHB) have short serum half-lives (~30-60 min); by 18 hours serum is typically negative even when urine remains positive. Serum is correct for early-window forensic testing (<6 hours), not at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).
rationales.C: Hair toxicology has a role but optimal timing is 3-5 weeks after exposure to allow the dosed segment to grow above the scalp. Hair is correct for retrospective single-dose detection, not at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).
rationales.D: NG aspirate is not standard in DFSA workup and is invasive without yield. NG aspirate is correct only in known recent acute oral overdose with bowel decontamination intent (RANZCOG sexual assault).
rationales.E: Oral fluid has a shorter detection window than urine and is not the preferred forensic toxicology matrix for DFSA at 18 hours. Oral fluid is correct for very recent (1-12 hour) exposures in some testing contexts, not at 18 hours (RANZCOG sexual assault).

rationales.A: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (carbamazepine) significantly reduce etonogestrel via CYP3A4; implant is UKMEC 3. Etonogestrel implant is correct only when not on enzyme-inducing drugs (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.B: Enzyme inducers reduce COCP efficacy and COCP is generally avoided <6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding due to milk supply and VTE concerns. COCP is correct only after 6 weeks postpartum, not breastfeeding-dependent, and not on enzyme inducers (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.C: POP efficacy is reduced by enzyme inducers; not recommended. POP is correct in non-enzyme-induced women preferring oral hormonal contraception (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.E: DMPA efficacy is not significantly reduced by enzyme inducers (UKMEC 1), so acceptable; however not the single BEST when IUD is suitable and woman wants long-term spacing. DMPA has bone density and weight effects. DMPA is correct when LARC is declined or implant/IUD contraindicated (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

rationales.A: LNG-IUD is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE; acceptable but Mirena insertion in anticoagulated patient requires care for procedural bleeding. LNG-IUD is correct in VTE history, but the question seeks the contraindicated option (LNG-IUD is not it) (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).
rationales.B: Etonogestrel implant is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE; usable with local bruising precautions. The implant is correct (acceptable) in VTE, so not the contraindicated option (FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.C: POP is UKMEC 2 in current/past VTE - acceptable. POP is correct (usable) in VTE; not contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.E: Copper IUD is UKMEC 1 in current/past VTE; non-hormonal so does not affect thrombotic risk. Copper IUD is the safest choice in VTE; not contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC).

rationales.A: Roux-en-Y bypass alters oral hormonal absorption and reduces COCP/POP efficacy; FSRH advises against reliance on oral methods after malabsorptive procedures. Oral methods are correct only after restrictive procedures (sleeve gastrectomy) without malabsorption (FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).
rationales.C: Oral COCP efficacy is reduced after RYGB; transdermal/vaginal CHC may be considered but post-bariatric weight loss instability and weight rebound make LARC preferred. CHC for cycle control is correct in non-bariatric patients (FSRH Overweight/Obesity).
rationales.D: Fertility returns rapidly postpartum (improves dramatically post-bariatric); contraception should be in place to avoid early conception (recommended 12-24 month spacing). Awaiting cycles is correct in non-fertile-period delays only (Family Planning Australia; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).
rationales.E: Pregnancy after bariatric is safe with planned spacing and nutritional surveillance; recommending sterilisation is not appropriate without patient-led discussion. Sterilisation is correct only when family is complete and patient-initiated (RANZCOG; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

rationales.B: CHC is contraindicated <6 weeks postpartum due to VTE risk regardless of breastfeeding (UKMEC 4 <3 weeks, UKMEC 3 from 3-6 weeks). Efficacy with bictegravir is preserved but postpartum VTE concern dominates. COCP is correct >6 weeks postpartum without other contraindications (FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.C: Efavirenz (NNRTI) is enzyme-inducing and REDUCES etonogestrel efficacy; adding it would worsen interactions. The anti-pattern is conditioning the implant on an interacting drug. Bictegravir as her current regimen preserves implant efficacy (FSRH Drug Interactions; ASHM).
rationales.D: Well-controlled HIV does not alter hormonal contraceptive efficacy; the relevant issue is specific drug interactions, not HIV. Hormonal avoidance is correct only with documented interacting ARV regimen (FSRH UKMEC; ASHM).
rationales.E: No contraceptive method is unsafe purely on basis of well-controlled HIV; reproductive autonomy and full method choice apply. Sterilisation is correct only when family complete and patient-initiated, never as 'only safe option' (ASHM HIV Management; UKMEC).

rationales.A: FPG >=7.0 and HbA1c >=6.5% in early pregnancy meets ADIPS criteria for diabetes in pregnancy (likely pre-existing T2DM), not GDM; treating as normal misses pre-existing disease. Re-screen at 24-28 weeks is correct only for FPG <5.1 with no diabetes-range values (ADIPS Hyperglycaemia in Pregnancy 2022).
rationales.C: FPG 5.1-6.0 with no diabetes-range result is GDM; this woman exceeds the diabetes threshold and HbA1c is also in diabetes range. GDM diagnosis is correct only when values are sub-diabetes-threshold (FPG 5.1-6.9 with HbA1c <6.5%) (ADIPS).
rationales.D: Delaying intervention by 4 weeks misses critical early-pregnancy organogenesis-period glycaemic control. Delay is correct only for clearly borderline single-test results with no clinical concerns (ADIPS; RANZCOG Diabetes in Pregnancy).
rationales.E: First-line pharmacotherapy in pregnancy for overt T2DM is usually metformin with stepwise insulin addition; insulin-only without lifestyle and metformin is not standard. Insulin-first is correct in severe hyperglycaemia (FPG >11) or metformin contraindication (ADIPS; RANZCOG; AMH).

rationales.B: TOP after 23 weeks is lawful in WA with two-practitioner assessment; interstate travel is not required. The anti-pattern is treating all advanced-gestation TOP as illegal. Interstate transfer is correct only when local provider declines (WA Act 2023).
rationales.C: No Australian state or territory requires partner consent for TOP. Partner consent is never legally required in Australia (WA Act 2023; equivalent state legislation).
rationales.D: WA's 2023 Act removed the previous mandatory waiting period and counselling requirement. 7-day waiting is correct only in jurisdictions with such legal requirements (WA Act 2023; SHQ WA).
rationales.E: Both medical and surgical TOP are available in WA; choice depends on gestation and clinical factors. Surgical-only is correct only when medical TOP is contraindicated or gestation is too advanced (SHQ WA).

rationales.A: No automatic paternal right to maternal health information; the woman is the patient and consent governs disclosure. Disclosure would breach NSW Health privacy and ADVO protective intent. Paternal disclosure is correct only with the patient's explicit consent (NSW Health DV Routine Screening; NSW Health Privacy Manual).
rationales.C: Transfer without patient agreement breaks continuity, undermines relationship she has built with the team. Transfer is correct only with patient agreement and clear safety benefit (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).
rationales.D: ADVOs apply across all locations as worded by the magistrate; hospital is not exempt. Inviting attendance contradicts the order. Attendance is correct only when the ADVO explicitly permits and the patient consents (NSW Police ADVO guidance; NSW DV Routine Screening).
rationales.E: Restricting care is unsafe and inappropriate; the response is to make care safe, not withdraw it. Care restriction is essentially never correct in IPV (NSW Health DV Routine Screening).

rationales.B: Misoprostol is third-line in Australian PPH/TOP haemorrhage protocols, used when first-line agents are unavailable or contraindicated. Misoprostol-first is correct only in low-resource settings without IV oxytocin (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: TXA is a useful adjunct (1 g IV within 3 hours per WOMAN trial) but does not address atony; uterotonics are the primary mechanism-targeted therapy. TXA-alone without uterotonic is correct only when atony is excluded (rare) (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial).
rationales.D: Carboprost is second-line after oxytocin and ergometrine; contraindicated in asthma. Carboprost-first is correct only after oxytocin failure in non-asthmatic patients (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Ergometrine/methylergometrine is standard second-line after oxytocin and contraindicated in hypertension/pre-eclampsia. Ergometrine-first is correct only when oxytocin is unavailable in normotensive patients (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: TXA is first-line medical therapy for HMB without structural cause, but empirical treatment in a woman >45 with IMB and unopposed-oestrogen risk factors without first excluding malignancy is inappropriate. TXA is correct after endometrial sampling excludes hyperplasia/cancer (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: She is in routine 5-yearly screening interval with recent negative HPV; bleeding pattern points to endometrial rather than cervical source. Repeat CST is correct with new cervical symptoms or visible cervical lesion (NCSP; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: MRI is second-line imaging used to map fibroids prior to surgery or when TVUS is inconclusive; not first investigation. MRI is correct after TVUS identifies a complex finding requiring better characterisation (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Hysteroscopy is indicated when TVUS shows focal pathology or when blind sampling is non-diagnostic; initial workup is TVUS plus Pipelle. Hysteroscopy-first is correct only when TVUS already shows focal lesion (e.g., polyp) (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

rationales.B: Hyperprolactinaemia typically causes secondary amenorrhoea with normal breast development and galactorrhoea, not primary amenorrhoea with dysmorphic features. Prolactin is correct in secondary amenorrhoea with galactorrhoea (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Ultrasound has already shown streak ovaries and a small uterus; MRI is reserved for suspected Mullerian anomalies when ultrasound is equivocal. MRI is correct for MRKH syndrome workup with uncertain US (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: GnRH testing differentiates pituitary from hypothalamic causes of hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism; the streak ovaries and dysmorphic features point to primary (gonadal) cause that karyotype diagnoses definitively. GnRH stimulation is correct in suspected hypothalamic-pituitary disease (ESHRE).
rationales.E: Anosmia raises Kallmann syndrome (hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism with normal ovaries and uterus); streak ovaries and short stature with somatic features are not Kallmann features. Olfactory testing is correct in suspected Kallmann (anosmia + delayed puberty + normal MRI brain) (StatPearls).

rationales.A: PCOS requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria; she has no hyperandrogenism and LH is low rather than the elevated LH:FSH typical of PCOS. PCOS is correct with oligo/anovulation + hyperandrogenism or polycystic morphology (CREPCOS 2023; Monash International PCOS Guideline).
rationales.C: POI requires FSH >25 IU/L on two occasions 4 weeks apart in a woman <40; her FSH is low and AMH preserved. POI is correct with elevated FSH and reduced AMH (Australasian Menopause Society POI).
rationales.D: Her prolactin is within normal range (typically <500 mIU/L); microprolactinoma usually causes elevations of 1000-5000 mIU/L with galactorrhoea. Microprolactinoma is correct with elevated prolactin plus galactorrhoea (Therapeutic Guidelines Endocrinology).
rationales.E: Asherman follows intrauterine instrumentation (D&C, especially postpartum or post-infection) with amenorrhoea despite a normal hormonal axis. She has no such history and gonadotrophins are suppressed. Asherman is correct with prior uterine instrumentation and normal hormone profile (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: She has had at least 4 episodes in 12 months, meeting criteria for recurrent VVC; isolated single-dose treatment without induction and suppression has high relapse rate. Single-dose fluconazole is correct for sporadic acute episodes only (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).
rationales.C: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis, not candidiasis; KOH confirms fungal infection. Metronidazole is correct in BV and trichomoniasis, never in confirmed candidiasis (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).
rationales.D: Diabetes is a risk factor but HbA1c 5.4% effectively excludes it; OGTT not warranted as a precondition for treatment. OGTT is correct only with HbA1c borderline or unexplained recurrent infections persisting after suppression (RACGP Red Book; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Intravaginal boric acid is reserved for non-albicans species (C. glabrata) or azole-resistant infection; her isolate is fluconazole-sensitive C. albicans. Boric acid is correct after speciation confirms non-albicans Candida (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

rationales.A: Surgery is reserved for women who have failed adequate conservative therapy including supervised pelvic floor training and lifestyle modification; first-line is conservative. Surgery is correct only after >=3 months supervised PFMT with persistent symptoms (RANZCOG; Continence Foundation Australia).
rationales.B: Oxybutynin is antimuscarinic indicated for urgency UI; history is pure stress incontinence so antimuscarinics will not help and may cause cognitive/anticholinergic side effects. Oxybutynin is correct in urgency-predominant UI (AMH).
rationales.D: Continence pessary may be useful for SUI but is second-line conservative after PFMT; first-degree prolapse is not the primary driver here. Pessary is correct as adjunct after failed PFMT or with prolapse contribution (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Urodynamics are not routinely required before conservative management of uncomplicated SUI; indicated when surgery is considered or diagnosis uncertain. Urodynamics is correct before surgical planning (NICE NG123).

rationales.B: Mesh midurethral sling implantation has been restricted by TGA in Australia following safety concerns; combined surgery is not first-line. Mesh sling is correct only after conservative failure with formal consent and TGA-compliant device choice (RANZCOG; TGA).
rationales.C: Abdominal mesh sacrocolpopexy is reserved for apical/post-hysterectomy prolapse, not first-line for stage-2 anterior compartment; restricted in Australia. Sacrocolpopexy is correct for refractory apical prolapse after conservative failure (TGA mesh).
rationales.D: PVR of 80 mL is mildly raised and likely improves with prolapse reduction; long-term catheter risks infection. IDC is correct only with refractory retention not improved by other measures (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Vaginal oestrogen is an adjunct to pessary tolerance but does not treat prolapse or SUI alone. Topical oestrogen is correct as adjunct to pessary use or for atrophic symptoms (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Although picture is consistent with endometriosis, fertility goals, deep endometriosis on examination, and pending STI screen require integrated specialist assessment; COCP suppresses fertility in someone trying to conceive. COCP is correct in endometriosis when not actively conceiving (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).
rationales.B: LNG-IUS is effective for endometriosis pain but is contraceptive and inappropriate while actively trying to conceive. LNG-IUS is correct in endometriosis pain management when fertility not desired (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).
rationales.C: GnRH agonists are second-line for refractory endometriosis; contraceptive and would delay fertility. GnRH agonists are correct in refractory endometriosis after first-line failure when fertility not immediate goal (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).
rationales.D: Laparoscopy is no longer required to diagnose endometriosis when imaging and clinical features are characteristic; preoperative MRI for deep disease mapping and multidisciplinary planning improves outcomes and protects fertility. Direct laparoscopy is correct only when imaging is inconclusive after MRI mapping (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Standard 0.5 mg is for low-risk women; sodium valproate is major teratogen requiring 5 mg daily. Low-dose folate is correct for women without teratogenic medications, prior NTD, diabetes, malabsorption, or BMI >30 (RANZCOG; NHMRC).
rationales.B: Iodine 150 microg/day is recommended pre-pregnancy but does not address dominant teratogenic risk from sodium valproate. Iodine alone is correct as standard pre-conception supplement, but inadequate as single intervention here (RANZCOG; NHMRC).
rationales.C: Seizure control does not mitigate teratogenic risk of valproate; the drug itself is the harm irrespective of seizure control. No-change is correct only when alternative AEDs have failed and informed decision is documented (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.D: She is already immune to rubella and varicella; live vaccines not indicated and contraindicated within 28 days of attempting conception. MMR/varicella is correct only with sub-protective titres and 4-week conception delay (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

rationales.A: Parental karyotype is not routinely first-line; reserved for couples with euploid first-trimester loss plus additional features (advanced maternal age, family history) or structural rearrangement on products. Karyotype is correct with structural rearrangement on POC or multiple losses with concerning family history (ESHRE RPL 2023; RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended in recurrent miscarriage outside research because results do not change management. Thrombophilia testing is correct only when there is a separate VTE indication, not for RPL alone (RANZCOG; ESHRE).
rationales.C: Hysteroscopy/SIS is reserved for cases where US is equivocal or suggests cavity abnormality; her TVUS shows a normal cavity. Hysteroscopy is correct when US is suspicious for intrauterine pathology (ESHRE).
rationales.E: NK cell testing is not validated for RPL and is explicitly not recommended by ESHRE and RANZCOG. NK testing is essentially never correct in routine RPL workup (ESHRE RPL 2023).

rationales.B: IVF is indicated after a defined cause is identified or following empirical workup; jumping to IVF without identifying treatable causes is inappropriate and ignores potential male factor. IVF is correct after workup identifies indications (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: She is over 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse and meets the definition of infertility; delaying investigation reduces chances of timely treatment. Continued attempts are correct only if duration is below the 12 month (or 6 month if >=35) threshold (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).
rationales.D: Tubal patency assessment is part of workup but follows initial investigations; laparoscopy as first-line is invasive and unjustified. HSG/laparoscopy is correct after initial workup is unrevealing (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).
rationales.E: AMH is part of broader workup but does not predict spontaneous conception; reassurance without male factor or tubal workup misses 50% of contributory causes. AMH-only is correct as adjunctive ovarian reserve assessment, never as sole investigation (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

rationales.B: Missing 3-5 school days/month, social withdrawal, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia is NOT normal and reflects high probability of endometriosis or other secondary cause. Reassurance is correct for mild primary dysmenorrhoea responsive to NSAIDs (Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Laparoscopy is not first-line in adolescents; empirical medical therapy is trialled first. Laparoscopy is correct after failed 3-6 month trial of medical management or suspected deep disease (RANZCOG; ESHRE).
rationales.D: GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory endometriosis after first-line hormonal failure; long-term use in adolescents has bone density concerns. GnRH agonists are correct in refractory disease with bone protection (ESHRE).
rationales.E: LNG-IUS is effective but is second-line after COCP trial in non-sexually-active adolescent; insertion may require GA. LNG-IUS is correct after COCP failure or when COCP contraindicated (Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Screening is for asymptomatic women without visible lesions; a fungating lesion mandates biopsy not re-screen. Re-screen is correct for asymptomatic women in screening interval (NCSP).
rationales.C: Postcoital bleeding with suspicious cervical lesion is not attributable to COCP; this delays cancer diagnosis. COCP cessation is correct only after cervical pathology excluded (NCSP; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: STI screen is reasonable but does not replace urgent biopsy of a visible suspicious lesion. STI swabs are correct as adjunctive workup, not as substitute for biopsy (NCSP; eTG).
rationales.E: Reassurance is unsafe given visible lesion and missed previous follow-up; cancer must be excluded. Reassurance is correct only after biopsy excludes malignancy (NCSP).

rationales.A: Ovarian torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency; every hour of delay increases ovarian necrosis and reduces salvage rate. Observation is correct only with low clinical suspicion and reassuring imaging (RANZCOG; KEMH).
rationales.B: MRI adds delay without improving on Doppler US findings that are already characteristic; diagnosis is clinical and US-based warranting immediate surgery. MRI is correct only when US is non-diagnostic and clinical suspicion is moderate (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Doppler signs do not reliably predict viability; ovaries appearing black often recover function after detorsion. Oophorectomy in adolescent should be reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy. Salpingo-oophorectomy is correct only with intra-operative confirmation of non-viability or malignancy (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital).
rationales.E: She has never been sexually active and imaging is diagnostic of torsion; antibiotics do not treat torsion and delay surgery. Antibiotics are correct in confirmed PID, never in torsion (Australian STI Management Guidelines; RANZCOG).

rationales.B: EUA is reserved for situations where examination is essential and cannot be tolerated; self-collection makes EUA unnecessary for screening. EUA is correct for symptomatic presentations requiring complete examination under sedation (NCSP).
rationales.C: Deferral perpetuates under-screening; self-collection answers this exact barrier. Deferral to clinician collection is correct only when clinician sampling is patient's preference (NCSP).
rationales.D: Urine HPV testing is not part of the Australian NCSP; validated self-collection is vaginal. Urine HPV is not currently approved in Australia (NCSP).
rationales.E: Vaccination does not eliminate the need for screening, which is recommended every 5 years from age 25 regardless of vaccination status. Vaccinated-no-screen is essentially never correct (NCSP).

rationales.B: 12-month repeat is for non-16/18 with NEGATIVE or pLSIL cytology; pHSIL escalates to direct colposcopy. 12-month repeat is correct for non-16/18 + negative/pLSIL only (NCSP).
rationales.C: No 6-month repeat interval in NCSP for screening results; colposcopy is required. 6-month interval was the old pathway; current NCSP uses 12-month surveillance only (NCSP).
rationales.D: pHSIL is treated as actionable and triggers colposcopy referral, not routine return. Routine 5-yearly return is correct only with negative HPV (NCSP).
rationales.E: Symptom-based delay is unsafe; pHSIL on screening requires prompt colposcopy regardless of symptoms. Symptom-based escalation is essentially never correct in screening-detected pHSIL (NCSP).

rationales.A: Three-dose schedule is reserved for immunocompromised individuals; immunocompetent people up to age 25 receive a single dose under 2023 schedule. Three-dose is correct only in immunocompromised patients (NCIRS HPV vaccination; Australian Immunisation Handbook).
rationales.B: Two-dose schedules were used prior to February 2023; current schedule is single dose for immunocompetent people aged 9 to 25. Two-dose was correct pre-Feb 2023 (NCIRS HPV vaccination).
rationales.D: HPV vaccination is most effective before HPV exposure; deferring removes protection during highest-risk period. Deferral is essentially never correct in HPV vaccination strategy (NCIRS HPV vaccination).
rationales.E: Cervarix (bivalent HPV 16/18) is not the funded NIP vaccine in Australia; Gardasil 9 (9-valent) is the program vaccine with broader genotype coverage. Cervarix is correct only in jurisdictions where it remains the program vaccine (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

rationales.A: Routine HPV screening is for asymptomatic women; symptomatic patients require co-test PLUS direct referral for colposcopy regardless of HPV result, because negative HPV does not exclude all cervical pathology in a symptomatic patient. Routine HPV alone is correct for asymptomatic women (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.B: Empirical antibiotics are not appropriate when STI testing is negative and symptoms have already failed empirical therapy; persistent postcoital bleeding with friable cervix requires structural assessment. Empirical antibiotics are correct in confirmed/strongly-suspected STI cervicitis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.C: Watchful waiting in a smoker with 4 months of postcoital bleeding and visibly friable cervix risks missing cervical cancer. Reassurance is correct only after biopsy excludes malignancy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.E: Breakthrough bleeding on COCP rarely presents with reproducible postcoital bleeding and friable cervix; structural pathology must be excluded first. COCP cessation is correct only after cervical pathology excluded (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: HMB with IDA and bulky uterus is non-specific and characterises benign causes (fibroid uterus) rather than locally advanced cervical cancer. This pattern is correct for fibroid disease, not for advanced cervical cancer (RANZCOG; Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.B: Postcoital bleeding with mobile cervix and normal renal function is more consistent with early-stage cervical disease or benign causes (ectropion, polyp); lacks features of local extension. Early-stage features are correct for FIGO IA-IB1 disease (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Postmenopausal bleeding with thickened endometrium is the syndrome of endometrial cancer, not cervical; cervical cancer typically presents with cervix-based symptoms. PMB+endometrial thickening is correct for endometrial pathology (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Pattern fits PID or tubo-ovarian abscess; locally advanced cervical cancer is typically afebrile unless superimposed pyometra. PID features are correct for pelvic infection, not cervical cancer (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Vaginal atrophy is common cause of PMB but cannot be assumed when endometrium is >4 mm; histological exclusion of endometrial pathology is required first. Topical oestrogen is correct after histology excludes pathology (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Any PMB warrants investigation; reassurance without histology is unsafe given endometrial cancer prevalence ~10% in PMB. Reassurance is essentially never correct in PMB without histology (RANZCOG; eviQ).
rationales.D: CA-125 is used in ovarian cancer risk stratification, not endometrial; histology directly addresses the diagnostic question. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Repeat imaging delays diagnosis without altering the indication for histology in PMB with endometrium >4 mm. Repeat US is correct only with inadequate initial imaging (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

rationales.B: LNG-IUS with surveillance is reserved for premenopausal patients wishing to retain fertility or unfit for surgery; in postmenopausal woman with completed family, definitive surgery is preferred. LNG-IUS is correct in fertility-sparing context (RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).
rationales.C: Oral progestogens are less effective than LNG-IUS for hyperplasia regression and not first-line in surgical candidate with completed family. Oral MPA is correct only in non-surgical candidates (RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).
rationales.D: Endometrial ablation is CONTRAINDICATED with hyperplasia + atypia or suspected malignancy because residual buried endometrium can harbour or develop cancer. Ablation is correct for benign HMB without hyperplasia (RANZCOG; RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).
rationales.E: Watchful waiting is unsafe for atypical hyperplasia given the high concurrent (~30-40%) and progression risk to invasive cancer. Watchful waiting is correct only in hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia in selected reversible scenarios (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

rationales.A: Colorectal cancer is on the differential, but palpable adnexal mass in postmenopausal woman shifts priority to gynaecological assessment. Colonoscopy is correct after gynaecological workup excludes ovarian pathology or in dominant bowel symptoms (RANZCOG; Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).
rationales.B: HE4 is not established Australian first-line marker; CA-125 is incorporated in RMI and Australian pathway. HE4 is correct in ROMA-based international pathways (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).
rationales.C: Antispasmodics in postmenopausal woman with palpable adnexal mass risks delaying ovarian cancer diagnosis; symptoms beyond 4 weeks warrant urgent investigation. Antispasmodic trial is correct only without red flags (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).
rationales.D: CT has a role in staging once malignancy is suspected, but pelvic US is recommended first-line imaging to characterise the lesion. CT is correct after US suggests malignancy (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

rationales.A: Delaying definitive assessment risks complete vascular compromise and ovarian loss; torsion is time-critical. Observation is correct only with low clinical suspicion and reassuring imaging (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).
rationales.B: US is the imaging of choice; CT adds delay and radiation without changing surgical management when US already shows enlarged ovary with reduced Doppler flow. CT is correct when US is non-diagnostic (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Even ovaries appearing dusky/black at surgery should be detorted and reassessed; oophorectomy as initial action sacrifices fertility unnecessarily. Salpingo-oophorectomy is correct only with confirmed necrosis/malignancy after detorsion (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).
rationales.E: Discharging a patient with suspected torsion is unsafe; emergency surgery is standard. Discharge is essentially never correct in suspected torsion (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Findings (figure-of-eight white atrophic change, architectural loss) are classic for lichen sclerosus, not generalised atrophic vaginitis; oestrogen would not address diagnosis or suspicious nodule. Topical oestrogen is correct for atrophic vaginitis with non-suspicious findings (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Ultrapotent topical steroids are mainstay for lichen sclerosus, but the new indurated nodule must be biopsied first because lichen sclerosus carries 4-5% lifetime vulval SCC risk. Steroid-without-biopsy is correct only when no suspicious areas are present (RANZCOG; BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).
rationales.D: Vulvectomy/wide excision is not standard treatment for lichen sclerosus; medical control with ultrapotent steroids is first-line and surgery is reserved for biopsy-proven malignancy. Wide excision is correct after biopsy confirms invasive disease (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).
rationales.E: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for refractory disease unresponsive to ultrapotent topical steroids; immediate priority is biopsy of suspicious nodule. Methotrexate is correct after biopsy and after steroid failure (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

rationales.A: Simple I&D without Word catheter or marsupialisation has high recurrence (up to 40%); the duct often re-closes once cavity collapses, particularly with prior recurrence. Simple I&D is correct as initial drainage when Word catheter unavailable (RANZCOG; KEMH Bartholin abscess).
rationales.B: Antibiotic monotherapy without drainage rarely resolves a tense fluctuant abscess; drainage is the primary intervention. Antibiotic-only is correct in early cellulitic phase without abscess collection (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).
rationales.D: Excision is reserved for recurrent abscesses unresponsive to drainage or for suspected Bartholin gland carcinoma (women >40 with solid mass); higher morbidity. Excision is correct after Word catheter/marsupialisation failure or with malignancy concern (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Conservative measures are insufficient for tense fluctuant abscess; while sitz baths help small early lesions, definitive drainage is required. Sitz baths are correct adjunct, not primary intervention (KEMH Bartholin abscess).

rationales.A: Annual HPV testing is not the NCSP test-of-cure pathway; structured ToC uses co-test at 12 months then yearly until two consecutive negatives. Annual HPV-only is correct in some immunocompromised surveillance, not post-LLETZ (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.B: Single co-test is not adequate; two consecutive negative co-tests at 12 and 24 months are required before returning to routine screening. Single co-test is essentially never sufficient (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.C: Clear excision margins reduce but do not eliminate the risk of recurrent HSIL; structured surveillance is required because residual HPV is strongest predictor. Routine 5-yearly is correct only after two negative ToC co-tests (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
rationales.D: Routine 6-monthly colposcopy is not the NCSP pathway after successfully treated CIN3 with clear margins; cytological/HPV co-test surveillance is sufficient. 6-monthly colposcopy is correct only with positive ToC results or persistent disease (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

rationales.A: Stopping tamoxifen unilaterally compromises breast cancer outcomes; substitution belongs to breast MDT after gynaecological diagnosis is established. Tamoxifen change does not address immediate need to exclude endometrial pathology. Tamoxifen switch is correct after gynaecological diagnosis established (eviQ Endometrial cancer; eviQ Tamoxifen).
rationales.C: Repeat imaging delays diagnosis without changing the indication for histology in symptomatic tamoxifen user; TVUS findings are poorly specific. Repeat TVUS is correct only with technically inadequate initial scan (eviQ Endometrial cancer).
rationales.D: Standard >4 mm threshold cannot be applied to symptomatic tamoxifen users; any PMB on tamoxifen requires histological exclusion. Reassurance is correct only after histology excludes pathology (eviQ Tamoxifen).
rationales.E: Asymptomatic surveillance of tamoxifen users with TVUS is NOT recommended in Australian guidance; surveillance does not improve outcomes. Bleeding, not thickness, is the investigation trigger. TVUS surveillance is essentially never correct in asymptomatic tamoxifen users (eviQ Tamoxifen; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Random FSH/oestradiol in a 44-year-old with regular cycles is unhelpful; cycle regularity argues against anovulation. Hormonal testing is correct only with established cycle irregularity (RANZCOG HMB).
rationales.B: Inherited bleeding disorder screen is for HMB since menarche or personal/family bleeding cues; her 8-month history without bleeding red flags does not prioritise this. Coag/vWF panel is correct with bleeding history clues (RANZCOG HMB).
rationales.D: Hysteroscopy is appropriate where TVUS suggests focal pathology or sampling is unsuccessful, but TVUS remains first-line. Hysteroscopy-first is correct only with TVUS showing focal lesion (NICE NG88).
rationales.E: CA-125 is non-specific in premenopausal women and has no role in HMB workup. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: GnRH analogues are used short-term (3-6 months) to shrink fibroids pre-surgery or to bridge perimenopause; not first-line ongoing therapy due to vasomotor symptoms and bone loss. GnRH is correct as bridge therapy, not chronic (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: TXA reduces blood loss by 40-50% but does not address bulk symptoms and is less effective than LNG-IUS overall. TXA is correct for acute heavy periods or as adjunct (NICE NG88).
rationales.D: NSAIDs reduce blood loss by ~25-30% and help dysmenorrhoea; useful adjuncts but not first-line for moderate-severe HMB with anaemia. NSAIDs are correct as adjunctive symptomatic therapy (NICE NG88).
rationales.E: Hysterectomy is definitive but should be reserved for failed/declined medical therapy; she has not yet trialled LARC. Hysterectomy is correct after failed conservative options or with patient preference (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Empirical hormonal therapy is now preferred first-line over diagnostic laparoscopy for suspected endometriosis. Laparoscopy is correct after failed medical therapy or for surgical planning (ESHRE 2022; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: CA-125 has poor sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis. CA-125 is correct in suspected ovarian pathology, not endometriosis diagnosis (ESHRE 2022).
rationales.D: MRI is reserved for suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis with surgical planning, not primary diagnosis. MRI is correct for deep endometriosis surgical mapping (RANZCOG; ESHRE).
rationales.E: GnRH agonists are second/third line after COCP/progestogens due to bone density and menopausal symptoms. GnRH is correct after first-line hormonal failure (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

rationales.A: POI diagnosis requires two FSH >25 IU/L at least 4 weeks apart; her first sample was 5 weeks prior, so confirmatory second now is appropriate. Reassurance with re-check at 6 months is correct only with isolated borderline FSH and no amenorrhoea (Australasian Menopause Society POI).
rationales.B: COCP can be used in POI but HRT provides more physiological replacement and better bone protection. COCP is correct when contraception desired alongside replacement (BMS).
rationales.C: Delaying oestrogen replacement until 40 risks cardiovascular, bone, and neurocognitive harm; replacement should commence at diagnosis. Defer HRT is essentially never correct in confirmed POI (ESHRE).
rationales.D: Adrenal autoimmune screen (21-hydroxylase antibodies) is part of POI workup but does not need to precede HRT commencement. Adrenal screen is correct concurrently, not before HRT (ESHRE).

rationales.A: Negative urine beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic; sensitivity approaches 99%. Ectopic is correct with positive hCG (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: PID presents with bilateral pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, fever, raised inflammatory markers; the sudden unilateral severe pattern fits torsion. PID is correct with sexually active patient with discharge, fever, bilateral pain (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.D: Ruptured cyst produces pain improving over hours; US shows free fluid without persistent enlarged ovary and Doppler is preserved. Ruptured cyst is correct with improving pain and free fluid alone (BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.E: Appendicitis progresses over 12-24 hours with central-to-RIF migration, anorexia, rising inflammatory markers; the abrupt severe onset with prior identical self-resolving episode points to torsion. Appendicitis is correct with progressive migrating pain (BMJ Best Practice).

rationales.B: Outpatient regimen is for mild-moderate PID without abscess or systemic features; her TOA, fever, tachycardia, vomiting require IV in hospital. Outpatient is correct in mild PID (RANZCOG PID; eTG).
rationales.C: Single-dose monotherapy is inadequate for any PID, let alone severe with TOA. Single-dose IM is essentially never sufficient in PID (eTG).
rationales.D: Surgery is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, ruptured TOA, or failure of antibiotic therapy at 48-72 hours. Surgery is correct after antibiotic failure or rupture (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Amoxicillin is not first-line PID therapy and does not cover gonorrhoea, anaerobes, or atypicals. Amoxicillin is correct in confirmed susceptible Listeria or strep infections, not PID (eTG).

rationales.A: LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1 in migraine with aura; local progestogen does not raise stroke risk. Mirena is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.B: Etonogestrel implant is UKMEC 2 in migraine with aura - acceptable. Implant is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.C: Cu-IUD is UKMEC 1 in migraine with aura. Copper IUD is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).
rationales.E: POP is UKMEC 2 in migraine with aura - acceptable. POP is correct in migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

rationales.A: CHC is UKMEC 4 <6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding, UKMEC 2 from 6 weeks to 6 months. CHC is correct >6 months postpartum or not breastfeeding (FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.B: LAM requires all three: amenorrhoea, exclusive/near-exclusive breastfeeding, AND <6 months postpartum; reliability falls thereafter. LAM is correct when all three criteria met (FSRH UKMEC).
rationales.D: Etonogestrel implant does not adversely affect milk supply and can be inserted at any time postpartum. Implant delay is essentially never required (FSRH; WHO MEC).
rationales.E: DMPA is UKMEC 1 from 6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding (UKMEC 2 before). DMPA is correct from 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (FSRH UKMEC).

rationales.A: Direct colposcopy is for HPV 16/18 regardless of cytology, or any oncogenic HPV with pHSIL/HSIL, or persistent HPV at 24 months. Direct colposcopy is correct in those higher-risk pathways (NCSP 2022).
rationales.C: 5-yearly screening resumes only after HPV becomes negative; any oncogenic HPV detection triggers 12-month repeat or colposcopy. Routine 5-yearly is correct only with negative HPV (NCSP 2022).
rationales.D: 6-month repeat is not NCSP-recommended; intermediate-risk repeat is at 12 months. 6-month repeat was the old pathway, now obsolete (NCSP 2022).
rationales.E: Treatment is based on histological diagnosis from colposcopy/biopsy, not on HPV status with negative cytology. LLETZ is correct after histology confirms high-grade dysplasia (NCSP 2022).

rationales.A: Endometrial thickness <=4 mm has very low cancer risk and TVUS alone may be sufficient initial triage; sampling not mandatory below this cut-off. Mandatory sampling at <=2 mm is over-treatment (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Although thin endometrium has high NPV, recurrent or persistent PMB requires sampling regardless of TVUS thickness. Reassurance without sampling is correct only for single PMB episode with thin endometrium and resolution (RCOG).
rationales.C: True but not directly relevant - she is not on tamoxifen. This option is a red herring (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: TVUS is appropriate first-line imaging; hysteroscopy is for thickened endometrium >=4 mm, recurrent PMB, or non-diagnostic Pipelle. Direct hysteroscopy is correct after TVUS shows thickened or focal endometrium (RCOG; RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Cyclical progestogen produces monthly bleed and is preferred in perimenopausal women still cycling; this woman is >12 months postmenopausal preferring no-bleed. Cyclical regimen is correct in perimenopausal HRT (BMS; Jean Hailes).
rationales.B: Oral oestrogen increases VTE risk ~2-fold; transdermal at standard doses does not. Oral CEE is correct only in women without VTE risk factors (BMS; Jean Hailes).
rationales.C: Tibolone is reasonable for vasomotor and libido symptoms but increases stroke risk (especially age >60) and is not first-line in women with elevated stroke risk. Tibolone is correct in younger women without stroke risk factors (BMS).
rationales.E: Unopposed oestrogen in intact uterus causes endometrial hyperplasia/cancer; progestogen mandatory. Oestrogen-alone is correct only after hysterectomy (BMS; Jean Hailes).

rationales.A: Low-dose vaginal oestrogen has minimal systemic absorption; recent observational data suggest no increased recurrence in non-AI users. Strict avoidance is correct only on aromatase inhibitor therapy or with patient/oncology preference (BMS; Jean Hailes).
rationales.B: Systemic MHT is generally avoided in breast cancer survivors; local low-dose vaginal oestrogen is more appropriate and effective for GSM. Systemic MHT is correct only in selected refractory vasomotor symptoms with oncology consent (BMS; Jean Hailes).
rationales.D: Vaginal laser therapy lacks high-quality efficacy/safety evidence; TGA and international societies caution against use outside trials. Laser is correct only in clinical trial settings (TGA; NAMS).
rationales.E: Ospemifene is contraindicated in current/recent breast cancer due to oestrogen agonist effects on breast tissue. Ospemifene is correct in postmenopausal dyspareunia without breast cancer history (FDA labelling).

rationales.A: Unhealed, indurated, or hyperkeratotic lesion in longstanding lichen sclerosus must NOT be assumed steroid-responsive; biopsy required to exclude differentiated VIN/SCC. Steroid increase is correct only after biopsy excludes neoplasia (BSSVD).
rationales.B: Topical tacrolimus is recognised second-line steroid-sparing agent, but priority here is biopsy of the suspicious lesion before changing therapy. Tacrolimus is correct after biopsy excludes neoplasia (BSSVD).
rationales.C: Lichen sclerosus carries 4-5% lifetime vulval SCC risk; long-term surveillance with prompt biopsy of suspicious lesions is recommended. Reassurance is essentially never correct in lichen sclerosus (BAD).
rationales.D: Diagnostic biopsy precedes any wide excision; excisional treatment depends on histology. Wide excision is correct after histological confirmation of malignancy (BSSVD; RCOG).

rationales.B: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 25 minutes is pathological; continuing oxytocin worsens foetal acidosis. Observation is correct only with normal/suspicious traces (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).
rationales.C: Category 1 CS is for acute decompensation (bradycardia, abruption, cord prolapse); pathological trace without acute decompensation warrants foetal scalp sampling first. Category 1 CS is correct with acute compromise (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Terbutaline tocolysis is indicated for tachysystole-related compromise; no tachysystole here. Terbutaline is correct in tachysystole-driven decelerations (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Membranes already ruptured and FSE without addressing the pathological pattern is insufficient. FSE alone is correct only with maternal-foetal CTG confusion needing direct trace (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Ephedrine is acceptable but contemporary obstetric anaesthesia favours phenylephrine because ephedrine crosses placenta and increases foetal acidaemia. Ephedrine is correct only when phenylephrine unavailable or maternal bradycardia (ANZCA).
rationales.B: Metaraminol is a reasonable alpha-agonist alternative commonly used in Australia, but phenylephrine has the strongest evidence base. Metaraminol is correct as second-line alpha-agonist (ANZCA).
rationales.D: Adrenaline boluses are reserved for cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis; this is treatable neuraxial hypotension. Adrenaline is correct in cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis only (ANZCA).
rationales.E: Maternal HR is 102; atropine treats bradycardia not hypotension and would not correct uteroplacental hypoperfusion. Atropine is correct in bradycardia-driven hypotension only (ANZCA).

rationales.B: Membranes already spontaneously ruptured; ARM not applicable. ARM is correct in intact membranes with poor progress (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Failure to progress requires demonstration after adequate uterine activity (after oxytocin); proceeding to caesarean before oxytocin is premature. Cat 2 CS is correct after oxytocin trial fails (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Starting at 6 mU/min risks hyperstimulation and foetal compromise; standard starts at 1-2 mU/min titrated every 30 minutes. High-dose start is correct only with maximum-dose review and continuous fetal monitoring in selected protocols (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Pain relief is reasonable but does not address slow progress with inadequate contractions; delay risks foetal compromise. Epidural without augmentation is correct when contractions are adequate (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Zavanelli is last-resort manoeuvre after failed internal manoeuvres and posterior arm delivery; not next step after McRoberts and suprapubic pressure. Zavanelli is correct as final salvage (RANZCOG; ALSO).
rationales.C: Fundal pressure is CONTRAINDICATED in shoulder dystocia; worsens impaction and increases uterine rupture and brachial plexus injury. Fundal pressure is essentially never correct in shoulder dystocia (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Deliberate cleidotomy is salvage manoeuvre reserved for failure of all other techniques. Cleidotomy is correct only when all other manoeuvres exhausted (RANZCOG; ALSO).
rationales.E: Episiotomy alone does not relieve bony dystocia; only creates space for internal manoeuvres. Episiotomy is correct as adjunct to internal manoeuvres (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Cord replacement is no longer recommended due to cord trauma and vasospasm; manual elevation of presenting part is preferred. Cord replacement is essentially obsolete (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top).
rationales.B: Oxytocin must be STOPPED immediately to reduce contractions compressing cord; 5-minute observation is unsafe with prolonged deceleration. Continued oxytocin is essentially never correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Tocolysis with terbutaline may be considered for hyperstimulation or transfer delay, but ventouse at -2 station is contraindicated (station must be >=+2). Tocolysis + ventouse combination is never correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Fundal pressure contraindicated; head is at -2 station with vaginal delivery not feasible. Category 1 CS required. Pushing-fundal pressure is essentially never correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Position is direct OA, not transverse arrest; Kielland's restricted to experienced operators for rotational deliveries. Kielland's is correct in deep transverse arrest by experienced operators (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG).
rationales.C: Sequential instrument use significantly increases neonatal morbidity (ICH, subgaleal); generally avoided. Sequential is correct only when head on perineum and primary instrument fails (RCOG).
rationales.D: Ventouse has higher failure rate than forceps in maternal exhaustion and suspicious CTG, plus higher cephalohaematoma/subgaleal rates. Ventouse is correct in early descent and adequate maternal effort (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: At +1 station with 0/5 palpable abdominally and direct OA, trial of instrumental is appropriate. Category 2 CS is correct after failed instrumental or with malposition not amenable to instruments (RANZCOG; NICE).

rationales.B: 15 minutes is idealised target rarely achieved; audited standard is 30 minutes. MTP should be activated proactively given suspected rupture. 15-minute target is correct only in immediate intra-theatre rupture (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: 60 minutes is threshold for category 2 CS; rupture is category 1 emergency. 60 minutes is correct for category 2 (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Epidural availability does not change category-1 timing; target is 30 minutes. 75 minutes is essentially never appropriate in suspected rupture (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: TOLAC is abandoned the moment rupture is suspected, not after delivery; target is 30 minutes. 45 minutes is appropriate only in category 2 status (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Praevia is suggested by low-lying placenta but classically causes painless bleeding without ROM as trigger; sinusoidal CTG immediately after SROM points to vasa praevia. Praevia is correct with painless antepartum bleeding without ROM trigger (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Abruption causes painful bleeding with tender, irritable, or tonic uterus; uterus here is soft and non-tender, no trauma/stimulant history. Abruption is correct with painful uterine activity (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Uterine rupture causes severe maternal pain, scar tenderness, maternal haemodynamic compromise, loss of foetal station; soft non-tender uterus and not in labour. Rupture is correct in TOLAC patient with these features (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Show is blood-stained mucous in small volume; moderate fresh bleeding after SROM with sinusoidal trace is not consistent. Show is correct for small mucus-blood loss (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Misoprostol is third-line in Australian guidance because of slower onset and higher pyrexia/shivering; ergometrine and carboprost are preferred when available and not contraindicated. Misoprostol is correct when ergometrine/carboprost contraindicated (RANZCOG PPH).
rationales.B: Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma; ergometrine is preferred second-line here. Carboprost is correct in non-asthmatic PPH (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Maximum oxytocin is 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's over 4 hours; doses above this risk water intoxication. Oxytocin escalation beyond max is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Balloon tamponade is appropriate after failure of medical management; uterotonics and TXA should be optimised first. Balloon is correct after first/second-line uterotonic failure (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Hydralazine is acceptable second- or third-line in Australian guidance, associated with more maternal hypotension and foetal distress than labetalol/nifedipine. Hydralazine is correct when labetalol/nifedipine fail or contraindicated (SOMANZ 2023).
rationales.C: Oral immediate-release nifedipine is acceptable first-line alternative to IV labetalol per SOMANZ, but carries overshoot hypotension risk. PO nifedipine is correct when IV access difficult (SOMANZ).
rationales.D: Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in pregnancy due to unpredictable absorption and severe overshoot hypotension. SL nifedipine is essentially never correct in pregnancy (SOMANZ).
rationales.E: Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis only and does not lower BP. Increased Mg risks toxicity. Mg dose change is correct for breakthrough seizure or sub-therapeutic levels (SOMANZ).

rationales.A: ECV for second twin has lower success and higher failure than internal podalic version with breech extraction; with back inferior and relaxed uterus, internal podalic version is preferred. ECV is correct with back-up presentation and superior position (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: ARM with transverse lie is CONTRAINDICATED due to high cord prolapse risk; version must occur first. ARM-first in transverse lie is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Caesarean for second twin after vaginal birth of first is associated with higher maternal morbidity than internal podalic version with breech extraction in experienced hands. CS for twin 2 is correct only after failed manoeuvres (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Delay risks cord prolapse on subsequent ROM, abruption from uterine retraction, and closing cervix; prompt management within 30 minutes is recommended. Waiting 60 minutes is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Only 5 mL of 1% lignocaine (50 mg) was used, far below toxic threshold (3 mg/kg without adrenaline); LAST presents with seizures and arrhythmia. LAST is correct with confirmed high-dose LA exposure (ANZCA).
rationales.C: No history of hypertension, proteinuria, or seizures preceding collapse; presentation is hypoxic collapse with coagulopathy not seizure. Eclamptic seizure is correct with PE history and seizure activity (SOMANZ).
rationales.D: PE is differential but does not classically cause early DIC and oozing from IV sites; AFE more consistent with full picture. PE is correct in unilateral chest pain or DVT history (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: ACS is possible but rare in a 36-year-old without risk factors and does not explain coagulopathy or temporal relationship with placental delivery. ACS is correct in older women with cardiac risk factors (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Benzylpenicillin alone is GBS intrapartum prophylaxis regimen and does not cover gram-negative or anaerobic organisms implicated in chorioamnionitis. BPG alone is correct in GBS prophylaxis only (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Pip-tazo is reserved for treatment failure or severe sepsis with broader gram-negative concerns; not first-line empirical. Pip-tazo is correct in severe sepsis or treatment failure (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Ceftriaxone + metronidazole is reasonable in penicillin allergy but not first-line in non-allergic. Ceftriaxone + metro is correct in severe penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy and adds little against typical chorioamnionitis pathogen mix. Vancomycin is correct in severe penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses dinoprostone in 24 hours; exceeding risks hyperstimulation. Third dose is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: ARM at Bishop 3 with high station is technically difficult and carries cord prolapse risk; cervix must first be ripened. ARM is correct when Bishop favourable (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Membrane sweep is for low-Bishop cervices >=1 cm dilated, but insufficient as single intervention after failed prostaglandins. Sweep is correct in latent labour with favourable cervix (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Caesarean is for genuine failed induction after both pharmacological and mechanical methods; she has not failed mechanical. CS is correct after both methods exhausted (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Hysterectomy is last resort even with completed family; conservative surgical measures recommended first if stabilisable. Direct hysterectomy is correct in life-threatening haemorrhage when uterine-sparing measures cannot be performed safely (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Haemodynamically unstable; transfer to IR outside theatre is unsafe. IR is correct in stable patients (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Bakri has maximum fill ~500 mL; overinflation does not increase efficacy. Observation in shock is unsafe. Re-inflation is essentially never correct in failed Bakri (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Internal iliac artery ligation is technically demanding, takes time, and reserved for after uterine artery ligation or by IR/vascular if available. Internal iliac is correct after B-Lynch and uterine artery ligation fail (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Although 3 hours is the threshold, descent has stalled at 0 station with malposition, caput, and moulding; continued pushing risks foetal compromise. Continued pushing is correct only with ongoing descent (RANZCOG OVB).
rationales.C: Rotational instrumental delivery requires experienced operator, often in theatre with trial-of-instrumental protocols; bypassing senior review is unsafe. Kielland's without senior review is essentially never correct (RANZCOG OVB).
rationales.D: Augmentation may be considered with descent and inadequate contractions, but with malposition at 0 station, caput, and moulding, oxytocin without resolution risks rupture. Augmentation is correct when descent + inadequate contractions (RANZCOG OVB).
rationales.E: Passive observation is reasonable BEFORE active pushing begins but not after 90 minutes with stalled descent. Passive observation is correct only at 10 cm dilatation before pushing begins (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: ARM is not feasible with closed cervix at -3 station and unfavourable Bishop 3; ripening required first. ARM is correct with favourable Bishop and accessible cervix (RANZCOG IOL 2021).
rationales.C: Oxytocin alone with unripe closed cervix is ineffective and associated with prolonged induction, exhaustion, and increased CS. Oxytocin is correct after ripening (RANZCOG IOL 2021).
rationales.D: Oral misoprostol IOL dosing is 25-50 microg every 2-4h; 200 microg is for PPH or TOP. 200 microg miso for IOL is essentially never correct (RANZCOG IOL).
rationales.E: Sweep alone unlikely to initiate labour with Bishop 3 at 41+2; post-dates beyond 41+0 warrants formal induction. Sweep is correct in earlier post-dates with favourable cervix (RANZCOG IOL).

rationales.A: Maternal age >=39-40 with multiple risk factors is used, but age alone is not strong stand-alone trigger at 39+0. Age alone is correct only with advanced age + other factors (RANZCOG; NICE).
rationales.C: Class I obesity without other risk factors does not by itself mandate induction before 39 weeks; supportive but not stand-alone. BMI 34 alone is correct only in research-grade evidence base (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Unfavourable cervix does not preclude IOL; ripening precedes oxytocin. Bishop >=8 requirement is essentially never correct (RANZCOG IOL).
rationales.E: ARRIVE suggested small CS reduction in low-risk nulliparas, but not primary evidence base in this high-risk multipara. ARRIVE evidence is correct in low-risk nulliparas (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Early decelerations are shallow, symmetric, mirror contraction, no shoulders; sharp drop within 20 seconds with shoulders is variable, not early. Early decelerations are correct with head-compression pattern (FIGO 2015).
rationales.B: Late decelerations have gradual onset (>30s to nadir), nadir after peak of contraction, return to baseline after contraction ends. Sharp drop with recovery before end is not late. Late is correct in uteroplacental insufficiency pattern (FIGO 2015).
rationales.D: Atypical variables show loss of shoulders, slow return, biphasic, or duration >60s; this description retains shoulders and recovers within contraction. Atypical variables are correct with concerning features added (FIGO 2015).
rationales.E: Prolonged decelerations last >3 minutes; these recover within 60 seconds. Prolonged is correct for >3 minute duration (FIGO 2015).

rationales.A: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability are pathological; continuing oxytocin worsens acidaemia. Continued oxytocin is essentially never correct in pathological trace (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).
rationales.B: Category 1 CS is for acute decompensation (prolonged bradycardia, abruption, cord prolapse); pathological without acute decompensation warrants intrauterine resuscitation + scalp lactate first. Cat 1 is correct in acute compromise (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).
rationales.C: Increasing oxytocin in pathological trace is contraindicated and worsens uteroplacental hypoperfusion. Oxytocin increase is essentially never correct in pathological trace (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).
rationales.E: Acute tocolysis is for tachysystole-related compromise; contractions are 5 in 10 (not tachysystolic >5/10/30min). Tocolysis is correct in tachysystolic patterns (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

rationales.A: Routine clamp-and-cut of tight nuchal cord before shoulder delivery is no longer recommended; converts contained circulation into acutely interrupted one if shoulder dystocia occurs. Clamp-and-cut is correct only as last resort with prolonged failure (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Forceful traction risks brachial plexus injury and does not address the cord; controlled somersault is correct. Firm traction is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Head is delivered; CS not feasible or indicated. Remaining task is to deliver body safely. Stop-pushing + CS is essentially never correct after head delivery (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Cutting cord during shoulder dystocia is hazardous - removes foetoplacental reservoir while foetus wedged. Cord cutting during dystocia is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Midline episiotomy increases risk of third- and fourth-degree perineal tears, especially with forceps - classic anti-pattern in Australian practice. Midline is correct in some US practices but not Australian (RANZCOG OVB).
rationales.C: Recommended angle is 60 degrees from midline AT crowning; 30 degrees and pre-crowning is incorrect. 30 degrees pre-crowning is essentially never correct (RANZCOG OVB).
rationales.D: Routine episiotomy is not recommended for spontaneous birth, but selective mediolateral is recommended for forceps to reduce OASIS. Avoiding episiotomy in forceps is correct only in selected low-risk cases (RANZCOG OVB).
rationales.E: J-shaped episiotomy is not standard practice and risks OASIS extension before the curve. J-shape is essentially never correct (RANZCOG OVB).

rationales.A: Second-degree tears involve perineal muscle but NOT anal sphincter; EAS involvement makes this third-degree (OASI). 2nd-degree is correct without sphincter involvement (RANZCOG OASI 2020).
rationales.B: 3a is <50% EAS thickness; this tear involves ~60% making it 3b. 3a is correct with <50% EAS (RANZCOG OASI 2020).
rationales.C: 3c requires IAS involvement; IAS intact here. 3c is correct with EAS + IAS torn (RANZCOG OASI 2020).
rationales.E: 4th-degree extends through rectal mucosa; mucosa intact here. 4th-degree is correct with mucosa involvement (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

rationales.B: Syntometrine contains ergometrine, CONTRAINDICATED in hypertension due to severe vasoconstriction and stroke risk. Syntometrine is correct in normotensive women (RANZCOG; eTG).
rationales.C: Ergometrine alone is contraindicated in hypertension - classic obstetric pharmacology anti-pattern. Ergometrine is correct in normotensive women (RANZCOG; eTG).
rationales.D: Misoprostol is second-line where oxytocin unavailable (home birth, resource-limited); oxytocin remains first-line in Australian hospitals. Misoprostol is correct as backup (RANZCOG; WHO).
rationales.E: Carbetocin is used selectively for elective CS in Australia, not routine vaginal birth. Carbetocin is correct in elective CS (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Retained placenta at 30 minutes with active management is the trigger for definitive intervention. Continued expectant is correct only with physiological management and no bleeding (RANZCOG; NICE).
rationales.B: Umbilical vein oxytocin has been studied but Cochrane and RANZCOG do not recommend as standard. UV oxytocin is correct only when manual removal cannot be arranged (Cochrane 2011; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Ergometrine causes tetanic uterine contraction trapping rather than expelling placenta - contraindicated for retained placenta. Ergometrine is correct in atonic PPH, not retained placenta (RANZCOG; AMH).
rationales.D: Even with epidural, manual removal should occur in theatre due to haemorrhage, inversion, and accreta risks. Bedside manual is correct only in extreme emergency without theatre access (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Pre-eclampsia diagnosed at or beyond 37+0 weeks is an indication for delivery in the same admission, not expectant to 39 weeks. Outpatient management is correct only in mild gestational hypertension without PE features (SOMANZ 2023; NICE NG133).
rationales.C: Bishop 1 does not mandate CS in non-severe PE with reassuring foetal status; ripening then induction is appropriate. Direct CS is correct with severe PE features or unfavourable foetal status (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Routine MgSO4 for all PE is not recommended in non-severe disease; reserved for severe PE or imminent eclampsia features. Routine MgSO4 is correct in severe PE only (SOMANZ).
rationales.E: Antenatal corticosteroids not indicated at 37+3 weeks; used between 24+0 and 34+6 weeks. Steroids are correct only in preterm gestation (RANZCOG corticosteroids).

rationales.A: She has documented non-severe penicillin allergy; cefazolin is appropriate. BPG is correct in non-allergic women (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Clindamycin is for severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy with documented isolate susceptibility; non-severe allergy uses cefazolin. Clindamycin is correct in severe allergy with susceptible isolate (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Vancomycin is third-line, only with severe penicillin allergy and clindamycin resistance or unknown susceptibility. Vancomycin is correct only after first/second-line agents excluded (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDC).
rationales.E: Oral antibiotics do not achieve reliable intrapartum levels for GBS prophylaxis; IV mandatory. Amoxicillin is also penicillin, contraindicated in allergy. Oral PCN-class is essentially never correct (Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Australian practice for term PROM does not support prolonged outpatient management beyond 24 hours; chorio risk rises with time. Outpatient management beyond 24h is essentially never correct in term PROM (Hannah TermPROM 1996; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Empirical antibiotics not indicated without clinical infection or risk factors. Antibiotics are correct with chorioamnionitis or ROM >18 hours with GBS-positive (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Digital VE after PROM should be deferred until labour is established or induction is planned; each exam increases infection risk. Digital VE is correct only when labour established (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Patient preference matters; immediate ripening is one option but not the sole appropriate plan. Direct PGE2 is correct only with patient preference (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Repeated forceps after failure with three correct pulls is associated with significant maternal/neonatal trauma; transition to CS required. Repeat forceps is essentially never correct after three failed pulls (RANZCOG).
rationales.B: Sequential instrument use significantly increases neonatal morbidity (ICH, subgaleal); contraindicated except when head on perineum. Sequential is correct only with head on perineum (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: CTG is pathological and pushing without instrument has failed for >100 minutes; expediting delivery in theatre is required. Push-without-instrument is correct only with non-distressed foetus (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Symphysiotomy is for shoulder dystocia or where CS is impossible (low-resource); not appropriate here. Symphysiotomy is correct in resource-limited settings without CS access (RANZCOG; WHO).

rationales.B: ECV has lower success rate than internal podalic version (~60% vs >90%); any delay risks cord prolapse, abruption, or cervical closure. ECV is correct in earlier antenatal management (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Combined vaginal-CS delivery is associated with worse outcomes for both mother and twin 2 versus internal podalic version; reserve CS for failed manoeuvres. CS is correct after failed manoeuvres (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Twin 2 transverse will not descend spontaneously; inter-twin interval should be as short as practical (commonly within 30 minutes). Waiting 60 minutes is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: ARM with transverse lie is contraindicated due to high cord prolapse risk; version must occur first. ARM-first in transverse lie is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Flucloxacillin would be first-line but is contraindicated with immediate hypersensitivity reaction to penicillin. Flucloxacillin is correct in non-allergic women (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Cephalosporins are avoided when penicillin reaction was immediate (Type 1) hypersensitivity with angioedema due to unpredictable cross-reactivity. Cefalexin is correct in non-immediate penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Amoxicillin-clavulanate contains penicillin and is contraindicated in immediate hypersensitivity. Augmentin is correct in non-allergic women with suspected polymicrobial infection (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: TMP-SMX is reserved for confirmed MRSA and avoided in mothers of infants <1 month due to theoretical kernicterus risk. TMP-SMX is correct in MRSA mastitis in older infants (Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.B: No fever, systemic symptoms, or defined erythema. Antibiotics are not indicated for inflammatory mastitis/ductal narrowing and may worsen dysbiosis. Flucloxacillin is correct in bacterial mastitis with fever/erythema (ABM 2022).
rationales.C: Aggressive massage worsens tissue oedema and risks abscess - the major anti-pattern that ABM 2022 specifically warns against. Aggressive massage is essentially never correct (ABM 2022).
rationales.D: Cessation worsens engorgement and inflammatory cycle; on-demand feeding remains essential. Pump-and-dump is correct only with documented contraindications (ABM 2022).
rationales.E: No fluctuance, abscess features, or failure of conservative management; surgical referral premature. Surgical referral is correct after failed conservative management or abscess (ABM 2022).

rationales.A: Drained collection requires drainage to resolve; antibiotics alone are inadequate and risk chronicity/fistula. Antibiotics-alone is correct only in early cellulitic phase without collection (ABM).
rationales.C: Surgical I&D is reserved for very large abscesses, multiloculated, or thinned/necrotic skin; leaves wound that may interfere with feeding. I&D is correct for large/complex abscesses (ABM).
rationales.D: Cessation worsens milk stasis and slows resolution; current guidance is to continue feeding or express. Cessation is essentially never correct in mastitis/abscess (ABM).
rationales.E: Antibiotic broadening without drainage is inadequate; established collection needs source control. Clindamycin alone is correct only without abscess collection (ABM).

rationales.A: Supplementation without indication suppresses prolactin-driven supply and undermines breastfeeding; growth and output are normal. Formula top-ups are correct with documented poor growth/output (ABA; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Galactagogues are considered only after structured assessment confirms inadequate supply, identifies reversible cause, and addresses technique. Domperidone is correct after structured supply assessment failure (ABM).
