rationales.D: Test-weighing is anxiety-provoking, prone to error, and rarely changes management with reassuring growth/output. Test-weighing is correct only in specific research/clinical questions about transfer (ABA; ABM).
rationales.E: No clinical suspicion for Sheehan (no severe PPH, no other pituitary dysfunction); imaging not indicated. Pituitary MRI is correct in suspected Sheehan or other pituitary disease (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Positioning already optimised with three lactation consultant reviews and infant growth faltering; further delay risks supply failure and breastfeeding cessation. Continued positioning is correct only with adequate transfer and growth (RCH; RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Mother wishes to continue and reversible structural cause identified; cessation not first-line when treatable lesion present. Formula transition is correct only after failed intervention or maternal preference (RCH; RANZCOG).
rationales.D: RANZCOG and RCH caution against indiscriminate posterior tongue tie or upper-lip tie release; evidence is weak and over-diagnosis documented. Posterior/lip-tie release is correct only with rigorous criteria and specialist assessment (RCH).
rationales.E: Shields already failed and do not address underlying restriction; reliance can worsen transfer. Nipple shields are correct as short-term adjunct, not long-term solution (RCH).

rationales.B: Fluoxetine and its long-half-life metabolite norfluoxetine accumulate in breastfed infant causing colic/irritability/poor feeding. Fluoxetine is correct only when patient previously responded well or sertraline not tolerated (LactMed).
rationales.C: Citalopram has higher milk transfer and more adverse infant reports than sertraline, plus dose-dependent QT prolongation. Citalopram is correct second-line after sertraline failure (LactMed).
rationales.D: Mirtazapine is reasonable when sedation/appetite dominant but data more limited; not first-line. Mirtazapine is correct when sedation/weight gain are therapeutic goals (LactMed).
rationales.E: Moderate-severe symptoms with EPDS 17 and passive ideation warrant combined psychological + pharmacological therapy; deferring risks deterioration. CBT-only is correct for mild symptoms or treatment preference (COPE).

rationales.B: DOACs not recommended in lactation; manufacturer data and LactMed advise against use. Apixaban is correct only outside breastfeeding (LactMed; AMH).
rationales.C: Rivaroxaban excreted into milk in animal studies; not recommended in lactation. Rivaroxaban is correct only outside breastfeeding (LactMed; AMH).
rationales.D: UFH is also compatible with breastfeeding but not preferred over LMWH for routine VTE outside renal failure or imminent procedure. UFH is correct in severe renal impairment or procedural bridging (AMH).
rationales.E: Aspirin not adequate for acute PE or DVT; therapeutic anticoagulation required. Aspirin is correct for extended secondary prevention only after initial therapeutic period (RANZCOG; eTG).

rationales.A: TGA and ABM safety guidance contraindicate domperidone with concurrent QT-prolonging medication and QTc >=450 ms; citalopram is QT-prolonging. Domperidone is correct only with normal QTc and no QT drugs (TGA).
rationales.C: TGA limits dosing to no more than 10 mg three times daily; cardiac risk dose-dependent. Plus active contraindications here. Higher dose is essentially never correct (TGA).
rationales.D: Metoclopramide has weaker galactagogue evidence and significant risks of extrapyramidal effects and depression with prolonged use. Metoclopramide is correct in short-term postpartum nausea, not long-term galactagogue (TGA).
rationales.E: Fenugreek has limited inconsistent evidence and is not a substitute for addressing milk removal or QT-drug review. Fenugreek is correct only as adjunctive lay-supported intervention, not medical recommendation (ABM).

rationales.A: Most dependent point is C (cervix) at +3, not Ba; anterior wall at Ba +1 is stage 2 in isolation but dominant defect is apical. Stage 2 cystocele is correct with anterior dominant + apex above hymen (IUGA).
rationales.C: Stage 4 requires most distal point at TVL -2 cm or beyond (essentially complete eversion); cervix at +3 with TVL 9 cm does not meet criterion. Stage 4 is correct in complete vaginal eversion (IUGA).
rationales.D: Bp is -2, well above hymen; posterior compartment is not the dominant defect. Stage 3 posterior is correct with Bp beyond hymen (IUGA).
rationales.E: Stage 1 requires most distal point >1 cm ABOVE hymen; cervix at +3 well beyond that threshold. Stage 1 is correct with all points >1 cm above hymen (IUGA).

rationales.B: Transvaginal mesh kits withdrawn by TGA in Australia in 2017 due to harm signals; not available. TV mesh is essentially never correct in current Australian practice (TGA mesh statement).
rationales.C: Sacrospinous fixation is reasonable native-tissue alternative for poor abdominal surgical candidates but higher recurrence than sacrocolpopexy. SSLF is correct in patients unsuitable for abdominal surgery (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Colpocleisis is obliterative procedure for older, frail, non-sexually-active women who accept loss of coital function; she remains sexually active. Colpocleisis is correct in elderly non-sexually-active women (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Already failed conservative with recurrent pessary expulsion and ineffective PFMT; another pessary unlikely to succeed. Pessary is correct as initial trial or in patients declining surgery (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: Cystocele causes URINARY splinting (anterior wall splinting to void), not posterior splinting to defaecate. Cystocele is correct with urinary splinting symptoms (IUGA).
rationales.C: Apical descent presents as dragging/heaviness; C -5 here well-supported, ruling out apical prolapse. Apical defect is correct with cervical descent beyond -1 (IUGA).
rationales.D: Enterocele may coexist but requires specific clinical or radiological identification; examination more consistent with rectocele. Enterocele is correct with peritoneal sac on imaging or characteristic finding (IUGA).
rationales.E: G3P3 with intact cervix on examination (C -5); has not had hysterectomy. Vault prolapse is correct post-hysterectomy only (IUGA).

rationales.A: Oxybutynin (especially IR) has highest CNS penetration; associated with cognitive decline in older adults. Listed as potentially inappropriate. Oxybutynin is correct only in younger patients without cognitive concern (Beers Criteria; STOPP).
rationales.C: Solifenacin is more bladder-selective with lower CNS penetration than oxybutynin but still anticholinergic burden best avoided in cognitive impairment. Solifenacin is correct in moderate OAB without cognitive concern (Beers).
rationales.D: Duloxetine is used for stress UI; diary shows no stress component. Duloxetine is correct in stress-predominant or mixed UI (NICE NG123).
rationales.E: Desmopressin restricted in older patients due to hyponatraemia risk; not first-line for OAB. Desmopressin is correct in younger patients with nocturnal polyuria and no hyponatraemia risk (Continence Foundation; AMH).

rationales.B: Surgery not first-line for mixed UI; stress-incontinence surgery may worsen pre-existing urge symptoms. Plus she declines surgery. Surgery is correct after failed conservative + pharmacological management (RANZCOG; NICE NG123).
rationales.C: Combined pharmacotherapy from outset not recommended; conservative first-line with single-agent for predominant component. Combined therapy is correct after single-agent failure (NICE NG123).
rationales.D: Urodynamics not required before conservative management of uncomplicated mixed UI; reserved for diagnostic uncertainty or pre-surgical planning. Urodynamics is correct pre-surgery (NICE NG123).
rationales.E: Continence pessaries useful mainly for stress UI driven by hypermobility; topical oestrogen for urogenital atrophy; neither addresses mixed UI primarily. Pessary is correct in stress UI with hypermobility (Continence Foundation; NICE NG123).

rationales.B: Adding pharmacotherapy for assumed OAB ignores high-volume retention with renal compromise; risks worsening retention and renal injury. Mirabegron is correct in confirmed OAB without retention (NICE NG123).
rationales.C: She has OVERFLOW incontinence from chronic retention, not stress; sling will precipitate complete retention and worsen renal injury. Sling is correct in confirmed stress UI without retention (NICE NG123).
rationales.D: Frusemide increases urine production into already obstructed bladder; worsens retention and AKI. Frusemide is correct in fluid overload only, never in obstructive uropathy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Outpatient delay unsafe with post-obstructive AKI with bilateral hydronephrosis; immediate decompression required. Outpatient delay is essentially never correct in obstructive AKI (NICE; Continence Foundation).

rationales.A: NICE NG123 advises against routine urodynamics before primary mid-urethral sling for clinically clear uncomplicated stress UI. Routine urodynamics-for-all is essentially never correct (NICE NG123).
rationales.C: Failed primary procedure is one indication but not the only one; urodynamics may be appropriate before surgery in complicated cases. Failed-primary indication is correct but not exclusive (NICE NG123).
rationales.D: PVR is one input; symptom pattern, prior surgery, neurological history, and prolapse all guide decision. PVR-alone trigger is correct only as one of multiple criteria (NICE NG123).
rationales.E: Pharmacotherapy for urge UI does not require urodynamics; conservative measures and empirical trial first-line. Urodynamics for any pharmacotherapy is essentially never correct (NICE; Continence Foundation Australia).

rationales.B: Urinalysis clear and clinical picture not UTI; antibiotics will not address fistula. Antibiotics are correct in UTI, not fistula (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.C: Urodynamics characterise bladder/sphincter function and not used to diagnose fistula. Urodynamics is correct in functional incontinence workup (NICE NG123).
rationales.D: MRI may help anatomical mapping for surgical planning of complex fistulae but not first step; dye testing plus cystoscopy is standard. MRI is correct in complex fistula surgical planning (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Continuous painless leakage after pelvic surgery with bladder dissection is fistula until proven otherwise; physiotherapy delays diagnosis. PT is correct in functional UI without fistula features (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: LS shows porcelain-white atrophic skin with figure-of-eight perianal involvement, labial resorption, clitoral hood phimosis; vaginal mucosa is NOT involved in LS. LS is correct without vaginal involvement (RANZCOG; BAD).
rationales.C: HSIL/uVIN shows discrete pigmented or whitish plaques with acetowhitening and HPV-related dysplasia on histology; inflammatory band and Civatte bodies favour lichen planus. HSIL/uVIN is correct with HPV-related dysplastic histology (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Zoon vulvitis presents with glistening orange-red patches without Wickham striae or oral lesions; histology shows dense plasma cell infiltrate. Zoon is correct with plasma cell infiltrate on histology (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: GSM produces diffuse pallor, loss of rugae, and dryness without erosions, Wickham striae, or oral involvement. GSM is correct with menopausal atrophic changes only (RANZCOG: Menopause).

rationales.B: HSIL/uVIN carries ~5-10% progression risk to invasive SCC if untreated; active management required. Watchful waiting is essentially never correct in HSIL/uVIN (RANZCOG; BSSVD).
rationales.C: Radical vulvectomy + lymphadenectomy reserved for invasive vulvar carcinoma, not preinvasive HSIL. Radical surgery is correct for invasive disease only (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Potent topical steroids are for inflammatory dermatoses (lichen sclerosus, lichen planus), not HSIL/uVIN; do not treat preinvasive dysplasia. Topical steroid is correct in inflammatory vulval dermatoses (BSSVD).
rationales.E: 5-FU largely supplanted by imiquimod due to high rates of vulval chemical burn and lower tolerability; indefinite application not recommended. 5-FU is correct in specific resistant cases under specialist care (BSSVD).

rationales.A: Simple I&D + packing has high recurrence rates (this is third recurrence); definitive procedure now needed. Simple I&D is correct for first acute presentation (RANZCOG; eTG).
rationales.C: Word catheter is acceptable first-line for primary or single recurrence; with three recurrences at same site, more definitive marsupialisation indicated. Word catheter is correct for first/second presentation (RANZCOG).
rationales.D: Abscess requires drainage; antibiotics alone do not resolve. Antibiotics reserved for surrounding cellulitis or sepsis. Antibiotics are correct as adjunct to drainage (eTG; RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Excision reserved for failed marsupialisation or suspected malignancy (usually women >40); higher morbidity. Excision is correct after failed marsupialisation (RANZCOG).

rationales.B: LAM reliable only up to 6 months, not 12; failure rate rises sharply once any criterion lapses. 12 months reliance is essentially never correct (FSRH).
rationales.C: Partial or mixed feeding (with formula or solids) breaks LAM criteria; reliability drops markedly. Reassurance with mixed feeding is essentially never correct (Family Planning Australia).
rationales.D: Return of menstruation INVALIDATES LAM; criterion is amenorrhoea since delivery, not presence of menses. LAM-with-menses is essentially never correct (Family Planning Australia).
rationales.E: CHC is UKMEC 4 in first 6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding (UKMEC 2 from 6 weeks to 6 months) due to VTE and supply concerns. COCP is correct >6 months postpartum or not breastfeeding (FSRH UKMEC).

rationales.A: Ceftriaxone monotherapy does not reliably cover anaerobes or enterococci; not recommended for endometritis. Ceftriaxone alone is correct in less complicated post-partum infections without endometritis suspicion (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.B: Flucloxacillin + metronidazole targets staphylococcal and anaerobic infection - inappropriate for polymicrobial gut/genital flora of endometritis; lacks adequate gram-negative cover. Flucloxa + metro is correct in selected wound infections, not endometritis (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.D: Pip-tazo offers broad polymicrobial cover but reserved for severe sepsis, HAI, or first-line failure. Pip-tazo is correct in severe sepsis or treatment failure (Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Septic with HR 108, fever 38.7, WCC 17.2, CRP 142; IV antibiotics and inpatient management required. Outpatient oral is correct in mild endometritis without systemic features (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: Flucloxacillin is penicillin and contraindicated in IgE-mediated penicillin allergy. Flucloxacillin is correct in non-allergic women (eTG).
rationales.B: Cephalosporins carry ~1-2% cross-reactivity in immediate-type penicillin allergy; should be avoided in confirmed anaphylactic history. Cefalexin is correct in non-immediate penicillin allergy (eTG).
rationales.D: TMP-SMX contraindicated in infants <2 months (kernicterus risk); at 3 weeks best avoided. TMP-SMX is correct in older infants with MRSA mastitis (eTG).
rationales.E: Clear bacterial mastitis (wedge erythema, fever 38.4, rigors) not responding to 36 hours of conservative care; antibiotics required. Conservative-only is correct in early inflammatory mastitis without systemic features (ABM 2022).

rationales.A: CS + BMI >30 + prior provoked VTE together exceed threshold for pharmacological prophylaxis on RANZCOG scoring; mechanical alone insufficient. Mechanical alone is correct in lower-risk patients (RANZCOG VTE).
rationales.B: 20 mg daily is standard for <50 kg or low-risk; this woman is 108 kg and high risk, requires weight-based dosing. 20 mg daily is correct in low-weight or low-risk women (RANZCOG).
rationales.C: Weight-based dose (80 mg for 91-130 kg) appropriate, but duration of only 10 days inadequate given prior VTE history. 10-day duration is correct in low-risk women without prior VTE (RANZCOG).
rationales.E: Warfarin compatible with breastfeeding but LMWH preferred postpartum because no INR monitoring and avoids early postpartum warfarin instability. Warfarin is correct in long-term anticoagulation with stable INR (RANZCOG).

rationales.A: CHC avoided in first 6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding (WHO MEC 3-4) due to theoretical effects on supply. COCP is correct >6 weeks postpartum if not breastfeeding (WHO MEC).
rationales.B: Does not meet LAM criteria because only partially breastfeeding; deferral risks ovulation. LAM is correct only with exclusive breastfeeding + amenorrhoea + <6 months postpartum (FSRH).
rationales.C: Cu-IUD insertion within 48 hours of delivery or after 4 weeks postpartum, so feasible at 5 weeks; however requires procedure scheduling. Cu-IUD is correct option when willing to wait for insertion appointment (WHO MEC).
rationales.D: LNG-IUS insertion follows similar timing; requires procedure scheduling and speculum work. LNG-IUS is correct option when willing to wait for insertion (WHO MEC).

rationales.A: Fluoxetine has longest SSRI half-life and long-lived active metabolite (norfluoxetine), leading to higher relative infant dose. Fluoxetine is correct when previously responsive or sertraline not tolerated (LactMed).
rationales.C: Paroxetine has acceptable breastfeeding data but associated with discontinuation syndromes; preferred when previously successful. Paroxetine is correct in patients with prior paroxetine response (COPE).
rationales.D: Venlafaxine is SNRI not first-line for new-onset moderate postnatal depression; reasonable second-line after SSRI failure. Venlafaxine is correct after SSRI failure (COPE).
rationales.E: Lithium generally avoided in breastfeeding due to high relative infant dose and infant toxicity risk (tremor, dehydration, thyroid); reserved for severe bipolar requiring lithium-based maintenance. Lithium is correct in bipolar with specialist input only (LactMed).
